2 Analysis sample setup

  • dat_main = fixed main sample from the cleaning script

  • ids_complete = exactly those individuals from this main sample

  • dat_main_final = the same dataset plus order_group

3 Order and carryover

3.1 Inspect session order structure

Check whether the order of the conditions is relevant:

  • whether participants were fasting or satiated first

  • whether order has a main effect

  • whether order moderates the effect of planned_condition

3.2 Crossover check

nn

2

20

planned_condition

satiated

10

=> balanced -> 10 fasted, 10 satiated per session

3.6 Order models

Test three models:
A) a baseline model without order;
B) plus the main effect of order;
C) plus the interaction between planned_condition and order_group.

-> If C does not perform better than B, there is no evidence that the condition effect depends on the order.

Data: dat_main_order
Models:
m_base: total_kcal ~ planned_condition + (1 | subject)
m_order_add: total_kcal ~ planned_condition + order_group + (1 | subject)
            npar    AIC    BIC  logLik -2*log(L)  Chisq Df Pr(>Chisq)
m_base         4 512.81 519.57 -252.41    504.81                     
m_order_add    5 512.88 521.32 -251.44    502.88 1.9329  1     0.1644
Data: dat_main_order
Models:
m_order_add: total_kcal ~ planned_condition + order_group + (1 | subject)
m_order_int: total_kcal ~ planned_condition * order_group + (1 | subject)
            npar    AIC    BIC  logLik -2*log(L)  Chisq Df Pr(>Chisq)
m_order_add    5 512.88 521.32 -251.44    502.88                     
m_order_int    6 512.59 522.72 -250.29    500.59 2.2907  1     0.1302
Linear mixed model fit by maximum likelihood . t-tests use Satterthwaite's
  method [lmerModLmerTest]
Formula: total_kcal ~ planned_condition * order_group + (1 | subject)

Interpretation

p = .1644 anova(m_base, m_order_add) is not significant –> no statistical evidence that the order has an additional main effect on total_kcal.

p = .1302 anova(m_order_add, m_order_int) is not significant –> no evidence that order moderates the condition effect

data:  d
W = 0.9649, p-value = 0.08184

-----------------------------
Check: Stress strong - mild 
-----------------------------
   Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
 -0.500   1.167   2.167   2.508   3.667   6.833 

Shapiro Wilk Test:

    Shapiro-Wilk normality test

data:  d
W = 0.95063, p-value = 0.01668

    Paired t-test

data:  stress_check_session$mild_mean and stress_check_session$baseline_mean
t = -6.6613, df = 59, p-value = 0.0000000101
alternative hypothesis: true mean difference is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 -1.712698 -0.921429
sample estimates:
mean difference 
      -1.317063 

    Paired t-test

data:  stress_check_session$strong_mean and stress_check_session$baseline_mean
t = 7.5496, df = 59, p-value = 0.0000000003169
alternative hypothesis: true mean difference is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 0.8755258 1.5070139
sample estimates:
mean difference 
        1.19127 

    Paired t-test

data:  stress_check_session$strong_mean and stress_check_session$mild_mean
t = 11.554, df = 59, p-value < 0.00000000000000022
alternative hypothesis: true mean difference is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 2.073907 2.942760
sample estimates:
mean difference 
       2.508333 

    Wilcoxon signed rank test with continuity correction

data:  stress_check_session$mild_mean and stress_check_session$baseline_mean
V = 168, p-value = 0.0000001043
alternative hypothesis: true location shift is not equal to 0

    Wilcoxon signed rank test with continuity correction

data:  stress_check_session$strong_mean and stress_check_session$baseline_mean
V = 1709.5, p-value = 0.000000005057
alternative hypothesis: true location shift is not equal to 0

    Wilcoxon signed rank test with continuity correction

data:  stress_check_session$strong_mean and stress_check_session$mild_mean
V = 1710, p-value = 0.00000000003753
alternative hypothesis: true location shift is not equal to 0

According to the Shapiro–Wilk test, the difference values for “mild” vs. “baseline” and “strong” vs. “baseline” did not differ significantly from a normal distribution (p = .124 and p = .082).

For “strong” vs. “mild” the distribution was not met (p = .0167 -> significant). -> The corresponding Wilcoxon test was also highly significant.

data:  d
W = 0.95298, p-value = 0.4435

-----------------------------
Check: Glucose fasted - satiated 
-----------------------------
   Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
 -2.900  -1.600  -0.800  -0.860  -0.325   1.500 

Shapiro Wilk Test:

    Shapiro-Wilk normality test

data:  d
W = 0.99006, p-value = 0.9982

    Paired t-test

data:  blood_wide$ketones_pre_fasted and blood_wide$ketones_pre_satiated
t = 1.8326, df = 18, p-value = 0.08345
alternative hypothesis: true mean difference is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 -0.01695152  0.24853047
sample estimates:
mean difference 
      0.1157895 

    Paired t-test

data:  blood_wide$glucose_pre_fasted and blood_wide$glucose_pre_satiated
t = -3.6318, df = 19, p-value = 0.001775
alternative hypothesis: true mean difference is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 -1.3556256 -0.3643744
sample estimates:
mean difference 
          -0.86

The metabolic state was more clearly evident in the blood for glucose than for ketones. Although ketone levels were in line with expectations, the evidence is not sufficient in this case. Glucose levels, on the other hand, differed significantly between the conditions.

9 Model specification

9.1 Random effects

First, use a random intercept as the main model

Then test the random slope

  • Only include the random slope if the model is stable

  • no major warnings appear

  • and the fit is visibly better

=> A random-intercept model was retained as the primary specification, as adding a random slope for planned_condition was not considered sufficiently justified given the small sample and two observations per subject.

9.2 Model helpers

Define reporting functions for mixed models

11 Moderation analyses

11.1 Moderation helper functions

11.1.1 Moderation 1: SRS

m_srs_add = main effect of Condition plus main effect of Moderator

m_srs_int = plus interaction; ANOVA tests whether the interaction provides additional explanatory power

# A tibble: 1 × 6
  n_rows n_ids n_nonmiss_outcome n_nonmiss_cond n_nonmiss_mod
   <int> <int>             <int>          <int>         <int>
1     40    20                40             40            40
# ℹ 1 more variable: n_complete_for_model <int>
Data: dat_main_srs
Models:
m_srs_add: total_kcal ~ planned_condition + srs_c + order_group + (1 | subject)
m_srs_int: total_kcal ~ planned_condition * srs_c + order_group + (1 | subject)
          npar    AIC    BIC  logLik -2*log(L)  Chisq Df Pr(>Chisq)
m_srs_add    6 513.09 523.23 -250.55    501.09                     
m_srs_int    7 514.68 526.50 -250.34    500.68 0.4181  1     0.5179
Linear mixed model fit by maximum likelihood . t-tests use Satterthwaite's
  method [lmerModLmerTest]

    514.7     526.5    -250.3     500.7        33

12 Sensitivity analyses

12.1 Mismatch check

What: Test robustness by excluding sessions with mismatches.

Why: This demonstrates that the main results are not driven by non-compliance. If I consider only the metabolically consistent sessions, does the main effect still hold?

# A tibble: 1 × 3
  n_rows n_ids n_missing_order
   <int> <int>           <int>
1     40    20               0
# A tibble: 1 × 4
  n_rows n_sessions n_conditions     n
   <int>      <int>        <int> <int>
1      2          2            2    19

Mit allen Sessions:
                        Estimate Std. Error       df   t value        Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept)             325.9398   48.06509 23.08195 6.7812152 0.0000006340661
planned_conditionfasted   2.0285   25.78292 20.00000 0.0786761 0.9380721112771
order_groupfasted_first  93.2855   65.48379 20.00000 1.4245586 0.1697026138224

Ohne mismatch Sessions:
                          Estimate Std. Error       df   t value      Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept)             315.815702   51.43830 21.81385 6.1396992 0.00000365489
planned_conditionfasted   3.755263   27.08204 19.00000 0.1386625 0.89117596795
order_groupfasted_first 102.546167   68.40194 19.00000 1.4991704 0.15026197953

12.2 Log transform

Log transformation typically:

  • makes large values less dominant

  • makes the distribution more symmetrical

  • often produces slightly cleaner residuals

What: Analyze log1p(total_kcal) as a robustness check. Does my main effect persist if I statistically reduce the skewness of the outcome distribution?

Data: dat
Models:
m0: f_m0
m1: f_m1
   npar    AIC    BIC  logLik -2*log(L)  Chisq Df Pr(>Chisq)
